planting.note: Direct sow in fall in moist, humus-rich, slightly acidic to neutral soil in a partially to fully shaded location; cold stratification over winter improves germination the following spring.

directions: Carolina Elephantfoot is a native aster-family perennial bearing large, rough-hairy, spatula-shaped basal leaves and wiry, branching stems that carry clusters of small, fuzzy, lavender-pink composite flower heads from late summer into fall. It thrives in moist, slightly acidic, humus-rich soil in partial to full shade, making it well suited to woodland gardens and shaded native plantings where few other natives will bloom. Direct sow seeds on the soil surface in fall and allow winter cold to break dormancy naturally; transplants are slow to establish and direct sowing is strongly preferred. Once settled, plants self-seed modestly and gradually colonize, providing late-season forage for native pollinators in otherwise dim corners of the landscape.
sources: North Carolina Extension Gardener Plant Toolbox

planting.note: Scatter seeds on bare, dry, disturbed ground in fall and press lightly into the soil surface; do not cover, as seeds need light to germinate.

directions: Purple Cudweed is a silvery-woolly native annual or biennial that forms a low basal rosette of soft, spatulate leaves coated in white wool before sending up dense leafy spikes of small rose-purple to brownish composite flower heads from spring through early summer. It is an important larval host plant for the American Painted Lady butterfly and attracts small native bees seeking pollen. Grow it in dry, sandy, lean, or disturbed open ground in full sun; rich or moist soils suppress growth and reduce self-seeding. Direct sow on bare soil in fall, pressing seeds lightly against the surface without burying them, and allow plants to complete their cycle and self-sow for a self-sustaining colony.
sources: Butterflies and Moths of North America

planting.note: Scatter seeds on bare, dry, sandy soil in fall; do not cover, as light aids germination, and avoid compacted or moist ground.

directions: Heller's Cudweed is a southeastern native annual everlasting that produces upright stems densely clothed in white woolly hair, topped in spring with clusters of tiny composite flower heads bearing papery, straw-white bracts that hold their form when dried. The entire plant emits a pleasant, mild balsam-like fragrance when brushed or crushed, making it useful in dried arrangements as well as native habitat plantings. Like other cudweeds, it serves as a critical larval host plant for the American Painted Lady butterfly. Sow seeds on the surface of dry, lean, sandy, well-drained soil in fall and leave them undisturbed through winter; plants self-sow freely in open, sunny ground and need no supplemental fertilizer or irrigation once established.
sources: Flora of the Southeastern United States
Butterflies and Moths of North America

planting.note: Scarify or nick seeds before sowing to break the hard seed coat and improve germination; sow after last frost directly in place in moist to moderately moist soil.

directions: Hemp Sesbania is one of the fastest-growing native annuals in North America, capable of reaching 6 to 12 feet in a single warm season with long, pinnate leaves and small, yellow, pea-like flowers that attract native bees from summer through fall. As a nitrogen-fixing legume, it builds soil fertility and produces exceptional biomass for use as a green manure when mowed or turned under before seed set. Scarify seeds by nicking or scuffing the seed coat before direct sowing after the last frost in moist, open ground in full sun. Cut or mow plants to the ground before seed pods ripen to prevent prolific self-seeding; incorporate the cut material into the soil as a rich green manure.

planting.note: Direct sow in spring after last frost in moist, sandy or loamy, slightly acidic soil in full sun; lightly scarify seeds before sowing for more reliable germination.

directions: Bladderpod Sesbania is a native annual legume of moist coastal plain habitats, ditches, and wet roadsides from Virginia to Texas, growing 4 to 8 feet tall with pinnate leaves and small, yellow, pea-like flowers in summer that are visited by native bumblebees. Its most distinctive feature is the inflated, papery, bladder-like seed pods that develop in late summer and rattle audibly when dry, making it easy to identify at maturity. Like other Sesbania species it fixes atmospheric nitrogen, building soil fertility in low, wet areas where most conventional cover crops fail to thrive. Direct sow lightly scarified seeds after the last frost in moist, slightly acidic, sandy or loamy soil, and mow plants to the ground before pods ripen fully to prevent excessive self-seeding the following year.
